nodes.skill_text: The bus stop shelter on the right with faded paint and a concrete structure is typical of rural Ukraine, Russia, or Belarus.
The car parked on the right side of the road faces away from the camera, indicating right-hand driving, common in much of the Americas.
The parking sign is European with a distinct blue circle and red cross, but the design is more common in Central and Eastern Europe.
The presence of above-ground power lines along the road is a feature commonly seen in rural Eastern European areas.
There are no visible road signs, but the combination of wooden telegraph poles and the fencing style reinforce the Scandinavian guess.
The white road delineators with a single black rectangle are characteristic of those found in Germany and the Czech Republic, specifically in rural areas.
No visible language or street signs, but the lack of Cyrillic with this architecture hints against Russia, Ukraine, or Belarus, narrowing it more toward Poland or perhaps the Czech Republic.
The curve and landscape of the road suggest a mountainous area, likely in the foothills rather than high altitude, which is common in southern Europe.
The road is very wide with a simple dashed white center line and solid white shoulder lines, a style common in Russia and nearby post-Soviet countries.
Given the mountainous backdrop and housing style, this is likely in a Bulgarian rural or peri-urban area not far from Sofia, possibly the town of Bankya.
The road has a rough, light-colored surface, common in rural areas of southern Europe, especially the former Yugoslav countries.
The apartment block visible in the background is of Soviet-era panel style, common in Ukrainian cities outside the very center.
